elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate (in)stabilities of periodic patterns under stochastic forcing in reaction-diffusion equations exhibiting a so-called Busse balloon.
elements.sent-2411-002: Specifically, we used a one-dimensional Klausmeier model for dryland vegetation patterns.
elements.sent-2411-003: Using numerical methods, we can accurately describe the transient dynamics of the stochastic solutions and compare several notions of stability.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, we show that stochastic stability heavily depends on the model parameters, the intensity of the noise and the location of the wavenumber of the periodic pattern within the deterministic Busse balloon.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, the boundary of the Busse balloon becomes blurred under the stochastic perturbations.. %.
elements.sent-2411-006: Self-organization leads to patterns in nature.
elements.sent-2411-007: Examples range from patterned vegetation in semi-arid regions~\cite{deblauwe2008global, bastiaansen2018multistability to mussel beds ~\cite{vandeKoppel2008experimental and bogs~\cite{couwenberg2005self .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: These pattern formation phenomena are often modelled with Reaction-Diffusion Equations (RDE) and they have been studied extensively, mainly in the deterministic setting~\cite{Murray2003MathematicalBiologyII,kondo2010reaction .}
elements.sent-2411-009: For a fixed set of parameters, the deterministic RDE models often support multiple stable and unstable spatially-periodic stationary patterns.
elements.sent-2411-010: The stable periodic patterns of these models are graphically represented via their wave numbers in so-called Busse balloons (with a system parameter on the horizontal axis, see Figure~\ref{fig:int:FromUnif for an example).}
elements.sent-2411-011: Busse balloons were originally introduced in the field of fluid mechanics to represent stable patterns of convection rolls~\cite{busse1978non .}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, nowadays, they are generic objects for spatially patterned systems.
elements.sent-2411-013: The Busse balloon can thus be used to predict which patterns are observable in a system.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, in any real-world system, noise will perturb these patterns.
elements.sent-2411-015: If the noise intensity is small, stochastic solutions will generally remain in the basin of attraction of deterministically stable patterns on long timescales, so the deterministic Busse balloon suffices.
elements.sent-2411-016: But for larger noise intensities this is not the case and what we mean by stability, observability, and the Busse balloon needs to be re-evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this article, we propose a framework to address the above-raised questions for RDE models influenced by noise, {i.e. for stochastic RDE models.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Although the techniques in this article are applicable to any stochastic RDE model that possesses a Busse balloon (in the deterministic setting), we focus on a model that models pattern formation in drylands.
elements.sent-2411-019: Drylands make up 41\% of the terrestrial land surface, with a human population of over 2 billion people~\cite{MillenniumEcosystemAssessment .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The presence of spatial vegetation patterns in these areas is ubiquitous~\cite{deblauwe2008global .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The patterns have on the one hand been interpreted as an early warning signals for critical transition~\cite{Schefferetal2009 and on the other hand have been linked to ecosystem resilience~\cite{Rietkerketal2021 , see also the recent review in~\cite{martinez2023integrating .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Since the creation of the first multi-component deterministic model for vegetation patterns by Klausmeier in '99~\cite{klausmeier1999 , that forms the basis of our stochastic model \eqref{eq:int:StochK below , many extensions of this model have been proposed.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The equations can be modelled in two space dimensions and with a (possibly varying) hill slope~\cite{bastiaansen2019stable , to include grazing~\cite{siero2018grazing or fluctuations in rainfall~\cite{eigentler2020effects , to name a few.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Similar models with three instead of two components include~\cite{HilleRisLambersetal2001,Giladetal2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: We study the influence of stochastic perturbations, or noise, on the stability and observability of periodic patterns in a one-dimensional non-dimensionalized Klausmeier model for dryland vegetation patterns on a flat terrain~\cite{klausmeier1999,siteuretal2014 : \begin{equation} du = [du_{xx}+a-u-uv^2]dt\,, dv = [v_{xx}-mv+uv^2]dt+\sigma vdW^Q_t\,.  \label{eq:int:StochK}\end{equation} The equation is posed on a spatial domain $[-L,L]$ with periodic boundary conditions for $t\in\R^+$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: The (nondimensionalized) biomass of the system is represented by $v(x,t)$ and $u(x,t)$ represents the (nondimensionalized) amount of water.
elements.sent-2411-027: The diffusion of water (in relation to the diffusion of the biomass) is modeled by the diffusion coefficient $d$ and is thus assumed to be large, $a$ models the (average) rainfall and $m$ is the mortality rate of the biomass.
elements.sent-2411-028: Here, we assume that this morality rate is noisy in nature, for example, due to human disturbances or grazing (however, see Remark~\ref{R:noise ).}
elements.sent-2411-029: That is, we replace the mortality rate $m$ of the deterministic model by $m+\sigma dW^Q_t$, which results in the multiplicative noise term (in It\^o interpretation) $\sigma v dW^Q_t$ in \eqref{eq:int:StochK .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Here, $W^Q_t$ is a Gaussian process on $L^2([-L,L])$ with no correlation in time, {i.e. white in time, and the spatial correlation is given by a Gaussian function $q(x-y)$, {i.e. coloured in space.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: This means that correlations of increments of the stochastic process $W^Q_t$ are given by $E[dW_t(x)dW_s(y)]=\delta(t-s)q(x-y)$, with $\delta(\cdot)$ the Dirac-delta function.
elements.sent-2411-032: See~\cite{lord2014book for an introduction to stochastic RDEs and related numerical techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In \eqref{eq:int:StochK we assumed the mortality rate $m$ of the biomass to be the only noisy term in the model.}
elements.sent-2411-034: We could also have included other stochastic processes in the model.
elements.sent-2411-035: For example, we could replace the rainfall $a$ with a stochastic process that actually models rainfall patterns in, say, Somalia~\cite{gandhi2023pulsed .}
elements.sent-2411-036: However, as the Klausmeier model is a conceptual model, including such detail in the model would force us to also significantly extend the other terms in the model (as was done in~\cite{gandhi2020fast,gandhi2023pulsed ).}
elements.paragraph-2411-001: Deterministic RDE model: stability and observability
elements.sent-2411-037: The deterministic version of \eqref{eq:int:StochK , {i.e. \eqref{eq:int:StochK with $\sigma=0$, has three spatially homogeneous solutions: the bare-soil state $(u,v)=(a,0)$ exists for all $a$; for $a\geq 2m$ two more solutions appear from a saddle-node bifurcation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: From these, the state $(u,v)=(a/2+\sqrt{a^2-4m^2 /2,(a-u)/m)$ is unstable and \begin{equation}\label{ss}(\bar u,\bar v)=(a/2-\sqrt{a^2-4m^2}/2,(a-\bar u)/m)\end{equation} is the one that is subjected to a supercritical Turing instability at some $a=a_T$.}
elements.sent-2411-039: For values $a$ below $a_T$, small amplitude patterns on top of this background state emerge, with wave numbers in the vicinity of the critical wave number.
elements.sent-2411-040: A subset of these patterns is stable and, thus, part of the Busse balloon of all stable periodic patterns.
elements.sent-2411-041: Close to equilibrium, the Busse balloon boundary is (in one space dimension) determined by the sideband instability~\cite{siteuretal2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Far from equilibrium, the boundary of the Busse balloon can be determined by means of continuation~\cite{RademacherSandstedeScheel2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: The Busse balloon for the deterministic version of \eqref{eq:int:StochK is given by the numerically computed red curve in Figure~\ref{fig:int:FromUnif .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Note that Busse balloons in general have to be computed numerically.

elements.sent-2411-055: For a periodic pattern with wavelength $\lambda$, the wave number is defined as $\lambda^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Given a periodic domain $[-L,L]$, a periodic pattern has $2L\lambda^{-1}$ maxima or, in the ecological interpretation, $2L\lambda^{-1}$ patches of vegetation.
elements.sent-2411-057: Throughout this whole article, we always choose $L=250$, hence without risk of confusion, we use the term `wave number' to mean $2L\lambda^{-1}$.

elements.paragraph-2411-002: Stochastic RDE model: first exit time and local wave number
elements.sent-2411-060: The stationary spatially-periodic patterns of the deterministic RDE model are no longer stationary solutions of the stochastic RDE.
elements.sent-2411-061: Furthermore, due to the structure of the multiplicative noise, only the homogeneous bare-soil state $(u,v) = (a,0)$ remains as background state.
elements.sent-2411-062: In a pessimistic view, one might say that this makes the notion of a {stochastic Busse balloon void.}
elements.sent-2411-063: However, as the numerical simulations will show, initial conditions that start close to a deterministic pattern will remain close to this pattern up to a certain random time, which we refer to as the {first exit time .}
elements.sent-2411-064: When a deterministically unstable pattern is chosen as initial condition, this first exit time can be very short, but for a deterministically stable pattern, it can be long, especially for small $a$-values and small $\sigma$-values.
elements.sent-2411-065: In other words, the concept of the first exit time in the stochastic case mimics the concept of stability from the deterministic case.
elements.sent-2411-066: Therefore, we use the {average first exit time to study the stability for periodic-like patterns in stochastic RDEs.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Note that the concept of average first exit times was already used in~\cite{arani2021exit as a measure of ecological resilience.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Average first exit times give an indication of the stability of the patterns but do not say anything about what happens after destabilization (similar to the deterministic case: the Busse balloon is related to stability, but not necessary observability, see Figure~\ref{fig:int:FromUnif ).}
elements.sent-2411-069: If the solution after destabilization is close to a periodic pattern, it will again destabilize after a certain waiting time.
elements.sent-2411-070: However, there is no guarantee that the solution is actually close to any of the periodic solutions.
elements.sent-2411-071: Hence, in order to talk about observability, {i.e. what are the typical properties of solutions we observe after a certain integration time, we must also be able to classify solutions that are not close to periodic patterns.}
elements.sent-2411-072: A first natural candidate for this is the {predominant wave number , i.e. the wave number where the absolute value of the Fourier transform attains its maximum.}
elements.sent-2411-073: However, the dynamics from one state, say with $n$ pulses, to another state, say with $n-1$ pulses, is -- already in the deterministic case -- slow, see Fig.~\ref{fig:DetLocal for an example.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Especially, the predominant wave number will only change from $n$ to $n-1$ after a long integration time.
elements.sent-2411-075: Therefore, it is {a priori not clear what wave number should be assigned to the solution during this {transient phase .}}
elements.sent-2411-076: In the limit of $t\to\infty$, this transient dynamics is not a problem, but on the short timescales of the stochastic dynamics, it is.
elements.sent-2411-077: Therefore, the questions about observability will depend on what definition of wave number we assign to a transient state.
elements.sent-2411-078: Another natural candidate for classification would be to count the number of maxima, which we refer to as the pulse number.
elements.sent-2411-079: However, just the pulse number does not tell us how these pulses are distributed over the domain.
elements.sent-2411-080: In Fig.~\ref{fig:DetLocal , the pulse number is fixed at 29, but the dynamics is driven by the fact that, initially, the pulses are not spread out evenly.}
elements.sent-2411-081: In the context of fluid dynamics, where the Busse balloon originates, the concept of a local wavelength is used to describe stochastic pattern formation in a Swift-Hohenberg equation~\cite{vinals1991numerical .}
elements.sent-2411-082: We will also use this concept of {local wave numbers to study the observability of periodic-like patterns in stochastic RDEs, as we will show that the local wavenumber captures both the quasi-periodicity of the patterns and the number of pulses.}
elements.sent-2411-083: This article is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-084: In \S\ref{sec:NumProp , we further introduce and define the stochastic concepts of (average) first exit time and local wave number to study the stability and observability of periodic-like patterns in stochastic RDEs.}

elements.sent-2411-086: We end the article in \S\ref{sec:disc with a discussion of the results and discuss possible avenues of further research.}

elements.sent-2411-273: We investigate the effect of stochastic forcing on the stable periodic patterns of the Busse balloon in a one-dimensional Klausmeier model for dryland vegetation patterns.

elements.figure-2411-028: \def\svgwidth{0.7\columnwidth} \caption{For a single $(k,a)$-pair where we can properly resolve $E[T_{\rm{exit}}(k,a,\sigma)]$, we can study the relation between $E[T_{\rm{exit}}]$ and $\sigma$. Here, we choose $(k,a)=(30,2)$. The value $k=30$ was deliberately chosen as it is the value for which $E[T_{\rm{exit}}(k,2)]$ attains its maximum for small $\sigma$-values. However, For $\sigma >0.2$, $k=31$ becomes the wave number with the longest average exit time. We plot on a $\log-\log$ scale the average first exit time as a function of $\sigma$ for $a=2$ and $k=30$.
